title: Iran Escalates Across Gulf States as Hormuz Standoff Deepens, Senator Graham Dies, Ukraine Reshuffles Government
subhead: Iran launches strikes on five Gulf nations and shuts the Strait of Hormuz as the US hits over 300 targets across Iran in three days. Senator Lindsey Graham dies suddenly at 71. Ukraine replaces its prime minister and signals a foreign policy pivot. Gibraltar removes its 118-year-old border with Spain.

sections.heading: Iran Strikes Five Gulf Nations and Closes Hormuz
sections.body:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps declared the Strait of Hormuz closed on Sunday and launched attacks on US bases and allied states across the Gulf — the broadest Iranian retaliation since the US-Iran conflict began. The IRGC said it struck the Prince Hassan Air Base in Jordan, destroying its command and control centre and MQ-9 drone hangars. The UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman, and Bahrain all reported responding to incoming missiles and drones. Qatar, a mediator in ceasefire talks, had not been attacked since April; the UAE since May — marking a deliberate widening of the target set.

The escalation came after the US hit more than 300 Iranian military targets across three waves, including coastal surveillance, logistics, communications, and naval assets in at least 10 provinces, according to Al Jazeera. US strikes also targeted a railway bridge in Golestan province near Turkmenistan — the deepest strike into Iranian territory since the April ceasefire — and the perimeter of Iran's only nuclear power plant in Bushehr without damaging it. Iran says 4,200 megawatts of electricity generation capacity have been destroyed, worsening a summer energy crisis as temperatures hit 40°C. One firefighter was killed in Chabahar, and fishermen have been reported dead or wounded in strikes on coastal infrastructure.

The US insists the Strait of Hormuz remains open. 'Iran was provided yet another opportunity to demonstrate adherence to the Memorandum of Understanding after being held accountable for earlier attacks on commercial vessels but has again failed,' Centcom said. Iran's parliamentary speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf wrote: 'The era of one-sided deals is OVER.' President Trump said the ceasefire was over but that mediated talks would continue. New Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who has not been seen publicly since succeeding his assassinated father, issued a statement emphasising revenge.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the widest direct Iranian military response of the war — hitting five Gulf states simultaneously, not just US assets. It signals that Iran's deterrence strategy now extends to the entire US-aligned Gulf security architecture, not just shipping lanes. The closure of Hormuz, if enforced, would affect roughly 20% of global oil trade.

sections.heading: Senator Lindsey Graham Dies at 71
sections.body:
Republican Senator Lindsey Graham of South Carolina died on Saturday evening from what his office described as a 'brief and sudden illness.' Preliminary findings from the medical examiner indicate a ruptured aorta. Graham had just returned from Kyiv, where he met President Zelenskyy on Friday. There were no known health concerns before the trip.

Graham, 71, was one of Washington's most influential foreign policy voices — a staunch supporter of Israel, military intervention against Iran, and arms for Ukraine. In one of his last televised interviews, he told CBS the US would 'obliterate' Iran if it did not submit to US control of the Strait of Hormuz. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu called him one of Israel's 'greatest friends.' Zelenskyy said he was 'deeply saddened.'

Graham's death reduces the Republican Senate majority from 53-47 to 52-47. South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster can appoint a replacement; a Republican primary is set for August 11. Trump told NBC he had 'somebody' in mind but declined to name a candidate. Graham's trajectory — from calling Trump a 'race-baiting, xenophobic, religious bigot' in 2015 to becoming one of his closest allies — tracked the transformation of the Republican Party itself.
sections.whyItMatters: Graham's death narrows an already thin Republican Senate majority at a moment when the Iran war and midterms are converging. His absence removes the Senate's most vocal Iran hawk and Ukraine advocate. The appointment and primary process will be a test of Trump's influence over the party's Senate composition.

sections.heading: Ukraine Reshuffles Government as Russia Exploits Air Defence Gap
sections.body:
President Zelenskyy announced a cabinet reshuffle on Sunday, replacing Prime Minister Yulia Svyrydenko — who held the role for a year and was credited with negotiating the critical minerals agreement with Washington. Zelenskyy said the shake-up is aimed at prioritising foreign policy, including domestic production of Patriot air defence systems, EU membership, and deeper Gulf ties. Svyrydenko said she was 'proud to have led the government during one of the most difficult periods in Ukraine's modern history.'

The move comes as Russian strikes continue exploiting Ukraine's critical shortage of Patriot munitions. Overnight Russian attacks killed three people in the Dnipropetrovsk region and one in Kherson, according to regional officials and Al Jazeera. Ukraine also struck back: a long-range drone killed a man in Russia's Samara region, and Ukrainian strikes on Russian-occupied Enerhodar killed four, including near the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant, according to Rosatom chief Alexei Likhachev.

At least 25 heads of state from the 'Coalition of the Willing' are due to meet in Paris on Monday to discuss supporting Ukraine and pressuring Russia to end the war. NATO's Ankara summit last week pledged more Patriot munitions, and Trump said he was open to licensing domestic production in Ukraine.
sections.whyItMatters: Zelenskyy's reshuffle signals a shift toward more direct foreign policy management at a time when Ukraine's air defence deficit is being punished daily. The Paris meeting on Monday will test whether the coalition can turn pledges into deliveries fast enough to matter on the ground.

sections.heading: Gibraltar Removes Border Controls After 118 Years
sections.body:
From July 15, the border fence between Gibraltar and Spain will be removed, allowing free movement of people and goods between the British Overseas Territory and the EU for the first time since 1908. The agreement aligns Gibraltar with the EU customs union and Schengen zone — a carefully negotiated solution to the unique post-Brexit challenge posed by a UK territory sharing a land border with the EU.

Gibraltar's Chief Minister Fabian Picardo called it 'complete and utter fluidity of people and goods' and 'a new dawn.' Spain's Foreign Minister José Manuel Albares described 'a new era.' Around 15,000 Spanish workers commute daily across the frontier, and the removal of the border — with its notorious rush-hour queues — is expected to boost business on both sides. La Línea de la Concepción, one of Spain's most deprived towns with nearly 30% unemployment, depends on Gibraltar for a third of local business revenue.

The deal comes with trade-offs. Gibraltar must comply with EU product regulations and introduce a 15% transaction tax (rising to 17%), replacing import duty. Some Gibraltarian business owners expressed nervousness about new paperwork and reduced competitiveness. But for a territory that voted 96% Remain in 2016 and endured a 13-year Spanish blockade under Franco, the agreement resolves a century-old friction point.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most concrete post-Brexit border solution anywhere — turning a hard border into no border by essentially folding Gibraltar into the EU's regulatory and travel framework while it remains British sovereign territory. It could become a template for other post-Brexit friction points.

sections.heading: Typhoon Bavi Pummels China After Killing 17 in the Philippines
sections.body:
Typhoon Bavi made landfall in China's Zhejiang province on Sunday — the second typhoon to hit the country in a week — forcing the evacuation of nearly two million people. Schools, work, and outdoor activities were suspended across the province, with 400 flights and dozens of train services cancelled. The city of Wenzhou, home to 10 million people, was directly in the storm's path. Authorities in Beijing ordered the evacuation of 100,000 more people to 'avoid risk.'

Before reaching China, Bavi killed at least 17 people in the Philippines via landslides, battered Japan's Sakishima islands with 144 km/h winds injuring at least five, and brought heavy rain to northern Taiwan. The storm began as a super typhoon with 290 km/h winds over Guam and the Northern Marianas last Monday. It has since weakened to a severe tropical storm but carries massive moisture.

This follows Typhoon Maysak, which struck southern China earlier in the week killing at least 39 people and spawning rare tornadoes in Hubei province. China is now dealing with compounding damage across its eastern and southern coasts.
sections.whyItMatters: Two major typhoons in one week, with Bavi starting as a super typhoon, underscore the increasing intensity of Pacific storms. Nearly two million evacuations, 56+ combined deaths across the Philippines and China, and compounding damage from Maysak create a humanitarian and economic challenge for Beijing alongside its geopolitical pressures.

sections.heading: South Africa Deports 53,000 in Anti-Immigration Crackdown
sections.body:
South Africa's government said more than 53,499 foreign nationals have been deported or repatriated in five weeks, the country's biggest such crackdown in years. Most were from Malawi, Zimbabwe, and Mozambique. Justice Minister Mmamoloko Kubayi said the operation also caught people wanted for alleged crimes, but warned protesters against conducting unauthorised searches of homes and businesses.

The deportations follow weeks of anti-immigration protests that saw violence, intimidation, and looting. Protesters set an unofficial deadline of June 30 for all undocumented migrants to leave. Several countries — including Ghana, Nigeria, Uganda, and Kenya — have flown their citizens home. The UN warned against scapegoating migrants for South Africa's socioeconomic problems, including roughly 30% unemployment.

President Cyril Ramaphosa acknowledged public concern but condemned attacks on migrants. The deportations reflect a broader continental tension between economic migration and national policy, playing out in Africa's wealthiest nation.
sections.whyItMatters: With 53,000+ deported in five weeks, South Africa's crackdown is one of the largest recent migration enforcement actions on the continent. The violence accompanying the protests — and the willingness of multiple African governments to organise repatriation flights — signals how economic pressure is hardening borders even in regions historically defined by cross-border movement.

bottomLine: Iran's simultaneous strikes on five Gulf states and the renewed closure of Hormuz mark the widest direct escalation of the conflict — no longer just a US-Iran confrontation but a regional one. Senator Graham's death narrows the Senate majority at a moment when war powers and midterms converge. Ukraine's government reshuffle and the Paris coalition meeting tomorrow will test whether the West can translate pledges into air defence munitions fast enough to close the gap Russia is exploiting daily. Meanwhile, two typhoons in a week and 53,000 deportations in five weeks are reminders that climate and migration pressures are producing casualty numbers that rival wars — and they're not waiting for diplomatic solutions.
